lessons.title: AWS Services Overview
lessons.overview: Understand how AWS names its services — by function, by underlying open-source technology, or with non-obvious names like Route 53. This naming pattern is a key exam strategy for eliminating wrong answers.
lessons.overviewVi: Tìm hiểu cách AWS đặt tên dịch vụ — theo chức năng, theo công nghệ open-source bên dưới, hoặc tên không trực quan như Route 53. Nắm được quy luật đặt tên là chiến lược quan trọng để loại trừ đáp án sai trong đề thi.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: How AWS Names Its Services
sections.contentHtml:
AWS offers hundreds of services across categories: compute, storage, database, networking, security, ML, IoT, and more. Service names often give you a direct hint about their category.

By functionAmazon Elastic Compute Cloud → compute service
By functionAmazon Simple Storage Service → storage service
By functionAmazon Relational Database Service → relational DB
Exam strategy: When you see a keyword in the question ("container", "compute", "storage"), you can eliminate options that clearly belong to a different category.
sections.contentVi: AWS cung cấp hàng trăm dịch vụ thuộc nhiều danh mục: tính toán, lưu trữ, cơ sở dữ liệu, mạng, bảo mật, ML, IoT và nhiều hơn nữa. Tên dịch vụ thường cho biết trực tiếp về danh mục: theo chức năng — Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud → dịch vụ tính toán; Amazon Simple Storage Service → dịch vụ lưu trữ; Amazon Relational Database Service → cơ sở dữ liệu quan hệ. Chiến lược thi: khi thấy từ khóa trong câu hỏi ('container', 'compute', 'storage'), bạn có thể loại ngay các đáp án rõ ràng thuộc danh mục khác.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Fully Managed Services
sections.contentHtml:
You will often hear "managed" or "fully managed" in AWS. This means AWS manages all underlying servers running that specific program — they handle maintenance, patching, and scaling so you focus on your application.

You doConfigure the service, manage your data, write your code
AWS doesServer provisioning, OS patching, hardware maintenance, scaling

Examples of fully managed services:

  • Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) — fully managed Kubernetes
  • Amazon Elasticsearch Service — fully managed Elasticsearch
  • Amazon FSx for Lustre — fully managed Lustre file system
sections.contentVi: Trong AWS, 'managed' hoặc 'fully managed' nghĩa là AWS quản lý tất cả máy chủ bên dưới — họ lo bảo trì, vá lỗi và tự động mở rộng để bạn tập trung vào ứng dụng. Bạn làm: cấu hình dịch vụ, quản lý dữ liệu, viết code. AWS làm: cung cấp server, vá lỗi OS, bảo trì phần cứng, tự động mở rộng. Ví dụ fully managed: Amazon EKS (Kubernetes được quản lý hoàn toàn), Amazon Elasticsearch Service (Elasticsearch được quản lý), Amazon FSx for Lustre (hệ thống file Lustre được quản lý).

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Naming by Open-Source Technology
sections.contentHtml:
Some AWS services are named after the open-source technology they use under the hood:

Amazon EKSKubernetes — open-source container orchestration for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containers
Amazon FSx for LustreLustre — open-source parallel file system (Linux + cluster)
Amazon ElasticsearchElasticsearch — open-source search & analytics engine on Apache Lucene
sections.contentVi: Một số dịch vụ AWS được đặt tên theo công nghệ open-source họ sử dụng bên dưới. Amazon EKS — chữ K = Kubernetes, nền tảng orchestration container open-source dùng để tự động hóa deploy, scale và quản lý containers. Amazon FSx for Lustre — Lustre = hệ thống file song song open-source (Linux + cluster). Amazon Elasticsearch Service — Elasticsearch = công cụ tìm kiếm và phân tích open-source xây dựng trên Apache Lucene. Hiểu nguồn gốc tên giúp bạn biết dịch vụ làm gì và liên kết với kiến thức đã có.

sections.label: Deep Dive
sections.heading: Non-Obvious Names: Route 53
sections.contentHtml:
Not all AWS names are intuitive. Amazon Route 53 is a DNS (Domain Name Service) that routes traffic through a cloud architecture.

Why "53"? In computer networking, DNS sends responses over UDP port 53. If the DNS message exceeds 512 bytes, it uses TCP port 53. The number 53 = the DNS port number.

Route 53 can route incoming requests to EC2 instances, S3, load balancers, and other origins.
sections.contentVi: Không phải tất cả tên AWS đều trực quan. Amazon Route 53 là dịch vụ DNS (Domain Name Service) định tuyến lưu lượng qua kiến trúc đám mây. Tại sao '53'? Trong mạng máy tính, DNS gửi phản hồi qua UDP port 53 (và TCP port 53 khi message DNS vượt quá 512 bytes). Con số 53 chính là số cổng DNS tiêu chuẩn — không phải ngẫu nhiên mà là tham chiếu kỹ thuật. Route 53 có thể định tuyến request đến EC2, S3, load balancers và các origin khác.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Exam Elimination Strategy
sections.contentHtml:
Use service categories to eliminate wrong answers:

  • See keyword "container" → think ECS or EKS, eliminate S3/RDS/EC2
  • See keyword "compute" → think EC2, Lambda — eliminate S3, Route 53
  • See keyword "relational database" → think RDS, Aurora — eliminate DynamoDB (NoSQL)
  • See keyword "serverless" → think Lambda, Fargate — eliminate EC2
  • See keyword "DNS / routing" → think Route 53
When a service name includes the category word (Compute, Storage, Database), that's your strongest clue.
sections.contentVi: Chiến lược loại trừ trong kỳ thi: thấy từ khóa 'container' → nghĩ đến ECS hoặc EKS, loại S3/RDS/EC2; thấy 'compute' → EC2, Lambda, loại S3, Route 53; thấy 'relational database' → RDS, Aurora, loại DynamoDB (NoSQL); thấy 'serverless' → Lambda, Fargate, loại EC2; thấy 'DNS / routing' → Route 53. Khi tên dịch vụ chứa từ danh mục (Compute, Storage, Database), đó là gợi ý mạnh nhất.

summary: AWS names services by function (EC2, S3, RDS), by open-source technology (EKS=Kubernetes, FSx=Lustre), or with non-obvious names (Route 53=port 53)
Fully managed = AWS handles underlying servers, patching, and scaling; you configure and manage data
Route 53 is named after DNS port 53 — the standard UDP/TCP port for Domain Name Service
Exam strategy: use service category keywords to eliminate wrong answers — container→ECS/EKS, compute→EC2/Lambda, DNS→Route 53
summaryVi: AWS đặt tên dịch vụ theo chức năng (EC2, S3, RDS), theo công nghệ open-source (EKS=Kubernetes, FSx=Lustre), hoặc tên không rõ ràng (Route 53=port 53)
Fully managed = AWS lo toàn bộ server bên dưới, vá lỗi và tự động mở rộng; bạn chỉ cấu hình và quản lý dữ liệu
Route 53 được đặt tên theo DNS port 53 — cổng UDP/TCP tiêu chuẩn cho Domain Name Service
Chiến lược thi: dùng từ khóa danh mục để loại trừ đáp án — container→ECS/EKS, compute→EC2/Lambda, DNS→Route 53

quiz.question: A solutions architect needs to recommend a DNS service to route users to the nearest AWS region for a globally distributed application. The team wants LEAST operational overhead. Which AWS service should they choose?
options: Amazon CloudFront with geo-restriction policies
Amazon Route 53 with latency-based routing
AWS Global Accelerator with endpoint groups
An EC2-hosted BIND DNS server in each region
quiz.explanation: Route 53 is AWS's fully managed DNS service — AWS handles the underlying DNS infrastructure, patching, and global availability, so the team only configures routing policies. CloudFront handles content delivery, not DNS resolution. Global Accelerator improves network routing but is not a DNS service. A self-hosted BIND server introduces significant operational overhead.

quiz.question: A startup is evaluating AWS services and notices that one service's name does not obviously describe its function. A developer asks why Route 53 is called 'Route 53' instead of something like 'AWS DNS'. What is the MOST accurate reason?
options: Route 53 was the 53rd internal AWS project when it launched
Route 53 uses 53 availability zones across its global network
Port 53 is the standard network port used by the Domain Name System protocol
Route 53 supports up to 53 different routing policy types
quiz.explanation: Route 53 is named after DNS port 53 — the standard UDP/TCP port that DNS servers listen on. This is an example of AWS naming a service after the underlying technology's well-known port rather than a descriptive function name. The other options are fabricated and do not reflect real AWS or DNS facts.

quiz.question: A company is migrating a containerized microservices application to AWS. The operations team wants to use Kubernetes for orchestration because they already have Kubernetes expertise. Which AWS service maps MOST directly to this requirement?
options: Amazon ECS with Fargate launch type
Amazon EKS
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with Docker platform
Amazon EC2 with Auto Scaling groups
quiz.explanation: Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) is AWS's managed Kubernetes service — the name EKS directly encodes the open-source technology (K8s/Kubernetes). ECS is AWS's proprietary container orchestrator, not Kubernetes. Elastic Beanstalk abstracts orchestration entirely. EC2 with Auto Scaling requires the team to install and manage Kubernetes themselves.

quiz.question: An engineer is reviewing AWS service responsibilities. Under a fully managed database service like Amazon RDS, which of the following tasks does AWS handle on the customer's behalf?
options: Defining database schemas and table structures
Writing SQL queries and stored procedures
Applying OS patches and database engine upgrades
Managing row-level security and application user permissions
quiz.explanation: With a fully managed service like RDS, AWS handles the underlying server hardware, operating system patching, and database engine version upgrades. The customer retains responsibility for schema design, query logic, and application-level access controls. This is the core definition of 'fully managed' — AWS manages infrastructure, you manage your data and configuration.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is reading an AWS whitepaper and encounters several unfamiliar service names. The architect knows that AWS sometimes names services after open-source technologies. Which of the following service names BEST demonstrates this naming pattern?
options: Amazon S3
Amazon EC2
Amazon FSx for Lustre
AWS Lambda
quiz.explanation: Amazon FSx for Lustre is named directly after Lustre, the open-source high-performance parallel file system. EKS (Kubernetes) follows the same pattern. S3 stands for Simple Storage Service, EC2 for Elastic Compute Cloud, and Lambda is an abstract function-based name — none of these reference open-source project names.

quiz.question: A company needs a compute service to run virtual machines for a legacy application that requires full control over the operating system, custom kernel modules, and specific hardware configurations. Which AWS service category keyword should guide the selection?
options: Container — pointing to Amazon ECS or EKS
Serverless — pointing to AWS Lambda
Compute — pointing to Amazon EC2
Storage — pointing to Amazon S3
quiz.explanation: The requirement for full OS control and custom kernel modules maps to the 'compute' category, specifically EC2 (virtual machines). Containers abstract the OS kernel, Lambda is serverless with no OS access, and S3 is object storage — none provide OS-level control.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is preparing for the SAA-C03 exam and wants to practice eliminating wrong answers using service category keywords. An exam question describes a scenario involving running containerized workloads on AWS. Which TWO services should the architect consider as likely correct answers based on category keywords? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon ECS
Amazon RDS
Amazon EKS
AWS CloudTrail
Amazon Redshift
quiz.explanation: ECS (Elastic Container Service) and EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) both fall under the container category. RDS is a relational database service, CloudTrail is an audit/logging service, and Redshift is a data warehousing service — none are container orchestration platforms and would be eliminated by the container keyword.

quiz.question: A company wants to host a relational database on AWS with LEAST operational overhead. The team does not want to manage server provisioning, OS patching, or backups. Which of the following BEST describes what they gain by choosing Amazon RDS over running MySQL on an EC2 instance?
options: The ability to run custom database engines not supported natively by AWS
Full root-level SSH access to the underlying database server
AWS manages the server, patching, and automated backups so the team only configures the database
Elimination of all database administration tasks including schema and query management
quiz.explanation: RDS is a fully managed service meaning AWS owns the underlying servers, applies OS and engine patches, and handles automated backups — the team configures the database, manages schemas, and owns the data. Root SSH access is not available on RDS. The team still performs database administration; AWS reduces infrastructure operations, not all administration.

quiz.question: An architect is troubleshooting a production issue where users report intermittent failures resolving the application's domain name. The operations team confirms the issue is at the DNS layer. Which AWS service should the architect investigate FIRST?
options: AWS WAF — to check for blocked DNS requests
Amazon Route 53 — to inspect DNS routing configurations and health checks
Amazon CloudFront — to verify origin domain configurations
Amazon VPC — to review network ACLs blocking port 443
quiz.explanation: Route 53 is AWS's DNS service, and DNS failures are investigated there. Knowing that Route 53 operates on DNS port 53 helps anchor this — DNS resolution issues start with Route 53 health checks and routing policies. WAF handles web request filtering, CloudFront handles content delivery, and VPC network ACLs on port 443 relate to HTTPS traffic, not DNS resolution.

quiz.question: A team is categorizing AWS services by their management model. They want to identify which services are 'fully managed' where AWS handles underlying server infrastructure. Which TWO of the following are examples where AWS manages the underlying compute infrastructure for the customer? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon EC2 with a custom AMI
Amazon RDS Multi-AZ deployment
AWS Lambda function
EC2 instance with self-managed PostgreSQL
Amazon EKS with self-managed node groups on EC2
quiz.explanation: RDS Multi-AZ and Lambda are both fully managed — AWS provisions, patches, and scales the underlying servers. With EC2 (options A, D), the customer is responsible for the OS and any software installed. EKS with self-managed node groups (option E) means the customer manages the EC2 worker nodes, making it only partially managed.

quiz.question: A developer joins a new team and sees AWS services named EC2, S3, and RDS in the architecture diagram. The developer is confused because these names do not describe what the services do. According to AWS naming conventions, what do EC2, S3, and RDS BEST represent?
options: Names based on the open-source technologies they are built on
Names based on the function or category of the service using abbreviations
Names based on internal AWS project codenames
Names based on the AWS region where each service was first launched
quiz.explanation: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud), S3 (Simple Storage Service), and RDS (Relational Database Service) are all abbreviated functional names — they describe the category or purpose of the service. This contrasts with EKS/FSx which reference open-source technologies, and Route 53 which references a technical port number.

quiz.question: During an SAA-C03 exam, a question asks which service to use when a company needs to 'run serverless functions triggered by API calls.' Using the exam strategy of service category keywords, which category keyword should guide the architect to the correct answer?
options: Container — leading to ECS or EKS
Compute (serverless) — leading to AWS Lambda
DNS — leading to Route 53
Storage — leading to Amazon S3
quiz.explanation: The keywords 'serverless functions' map directly to the serverless compute category, which points to AWS Lambda. ECS/EKS are for containerized workloads, Route 53 is DNS, and S3 is object storage. Using category keywords to eliminate wrong answers is a core SAA-C03 exam strategy.

quiz.question: A company runs a high-performance computing (HPC) workload that requires a parallel file system with high throughput and low latency shared storage across many compute nodes. A solutions architect recognizes the service name references the open-source file system it is built on. Which AWS service should they recommend?
options: Amazon EFS — Elastic File System
Amazon S3 with Transfer Acceleration
Amazon FSx for Lustre
Amazon EBS with Provisioned IOPS
quiz.explanation: Amazon FSx for Lustre is named after the Lustre open-source parallel file system, specifically designed for HPC workloads requiring high throughput shared storage. EFS is a general-purpose NFS file system, S3 is object storage (not a file system for HPC), and EBS is block storage attached to a single EC2 instance.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is studying how AWS names its services. Which THREE of the following statements accurately describe AWS service naming patterns? (Choose THREE)
options: Some services are named after their function using abbreviations, such as EC2 for Elastic Compute Cloud
Some services are named after the open-source technology they are based on, such as EKS referencing Kubernetes
All AWS services use descriptive English names that fully spell out their purpose
Some services have non-obvious names tied to a technical detail, such as Route 53 referencing DNS port 53
AWS never reuses naming conventions across different service families
quiz.explanation: AWS uses at least three naming patterns: functional abbreviations (EC2, S3, RDS), open-source technology names (EKS=Kubernetes, FSx=Lustre), and non-obvious technical references (Route 53=port 53). Not all services have fully descriptive names (Lambda, Glacier are abstract), and AWS does reuse naming patterns across service families.

quiz.question: An architect is evaluating two options for running a Kubernetes-based application on AWS. Option A: Use Amazon EKS. Option B: Launch EC2 instances and manually install and configure a Kubernetes control plane. From a 'fully managed' service perspective, what is the PRIMARY advantage of Option A?
options: EKS allows more Kubernetes version choices than a self-managed installation
EKS provides lower network latency between pods than self-managed Kubernetes
EKS has AWS manage the Kubernetes control plane including patching and availability, reducing operational burden
EKS eliminates the need to manage worker node capacity and scaling
quiz.explanation: EKS is a fully managed Kubernetes service where AWS manages the Kubernetes control plane — patching, version upgrades, and high availability of the control plane components. With self-managed Kubernetes on EC2, the team is responsible for all of this. Worker nodes (data plane) still require management in standard EKS, making option D partially incorrect.

quiz.question: A financial services company is designing an architecture on AWS. Their security team asks: 'For our Amazon RDS database, who is responsible for network-level OS patching of the underlying database server?' What is the correct answer?
options: The customer, because they own all data and configurations on AWS
AWS, because RDS is a fully managed service where AWS handles underlying server patching
A shared responsibility where both AWS and the customer apply patches simultaneously
A third-party vendor selected from AWS Marketplace
quiz.explanation: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, fully managed services like RDS shift OS and infrastructure patching to AWS. The customer is responsible for the database itself — schemas, data, users, and application-level security. The customer does NOT patch the underlying RDS server OS.

quiz.question: An exam question on SAA-C03 describes this scenario: 'A company needs to migrate their on-premises DNS infrastructure to AWS with high availability and automatic failover.' A test-taker uses the service category keyword strategy. Which category keyword should they identify FIRST to narrow down the correct service?
options: Compute, narrowing to EC2 or Lambda
DNS, narrowing to Route 53
Container, narrowing to ECS or EKS
Storage, narrowing to S3 or EFS
quiz.explanation: The scenario explicitly involves DNS infrastructure migration, so the DNS category keyword directly points to Route 53 as the correct service. This demonstrates the exam strategy: identify the category keyword in the scenario description first, then eliminate services outside that category before evaluating the remaining options.

quiz.question: A solutions architect needs to select services for a new application with these requirements: (1) run containerized microservices, and (2) store relational data with LEAST operational overhead. Which TWO AWS services BEST match these requirements? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon ECS for container orchestration
Amazon EC2 with self-managed Docker for containers
Amazon RDS for the relational database
Amazon DynamoDB for the relational database
Amazon Redshift for the relational database
quiz.explanation: ECS (container category) handles containerized microservices with less operational overhead than self-managed Docker on EC2. RDS (relational database category) is the fully managed relational database service, matching both the data type requirement and least operational overhead. DynamoDB is NoSQL (not relational), and Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics — both are wrong data model choices.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is explaining to a junior engineer why understanding AWS service naming conventions matters for the SAA-C03 exam. The architect gives this advice: 'When you see an unfamiliar AWS service name, try to decode it — it often tells you what category the service is in.' Which of the following examples BEST supports this advice?
options: AWS Lambda — the name Lambda clearly indicates it is a serverless function service
Amazon EKS — the 'K' in EKS signals a connection to Kubernetes, indicating it is a container orchestration service
Amazon Glacier — the name Glacier clearly communicates it is an archival storage service
Amazon Aurora — the name Aurora directly describes it as a high-performance relational database
quiz.explanation: EKS contains 'K' referencing Kubernetes (often abbreviated K8s), making the open-source technology embedded in the name a direct signal of the service category — container orchestration. Lambda is a mathematical/CS term that doesn't obviously indicate serverless, Glacier suggests cold/slow storage only by metaphor, and Aurora is a completely abstract name with no inherent technical meaning.

quiz.question: A company is planning to move all DNS management to AWS. A network engineer mentions that the AWS DNS service name hints at which network port it uses. Which port does DNS operate on, and why is this relevant to identifying the correct AWS service?
options: Port 80 — HTTP traffic, which Route 53 uses to serve DNS over HTTP
Port 443 — HTTPS traffic, which Route 53 uses for encrypted DNS queries
Port 53 — the standard DNS port, which Route 53 is named after to signal it is the DNS service
Port 22 — SSH traffic, which Route 53 uses to manage DNS server configurations
quiz.explanation: DNS uses port 53 (both UDP and TCP), and Route 53 is explicitly named after this port — making it recognizable as the DNS service even without knowing the full service description. This is an example of a non-obvious AWS name that encodes a technical detail. Port 80 is HTTP, port 443 is HTTPS, and port 22 is SSH — none are DNS ports.

lessons.title: AWS Compute Services
lessons.overview: Explore AWS compute services across 4 categories: virtual machines (EC2, LightSail), serverless (Lambda), orchestration (Batch, Elastic Beanstalk), and hybrid (Outposts). Learn when to use each and their key differences.
lessons.overviewVi: Khám phá các dịch vụ tính toán AWS trên 4 nhóm: máy ảo (EC2, LightSail), serverless (Lambda), orchestration (Batch, Elastic Beanstalk) và hybrid (Outposts). Tìm hiểu khi nào dùng từng loại và điểm khác biệt chính.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: 4 Compute Categories
sections.contentHtml:
AWS compute services are grouped into four types:

Virtual MachinesEC2, Amazon LightSail — you choose OS, CPU, RAM, storage
ServerlessAWS Lambda — upload code, AWS manages everything
OrchestrationAWS Batch, Elastic Beanstalk — coordinate EC2 fleets
HybridAWS Outposts — run AWS on your own data center
Container services (ECS, EKS, Fargate) are the 4th group covered in the next lesson.
sections.contentVi: Các dịch vụ tính toán AWS được nhóm thành 4 loại: Máy ảo (Virtual Machines) — EC2 và Amazon LightSail, cho phép chọn OS, CPU, RAM, lưu trữ. Serverless — AWS Lambda, chỉ cần upload code, AWS lo toàn bộ. Orchestration — AWS Batch và Elastic Beanstalk điều phối đội máy EC2. Hybrid — AWS Outposts chạy AWS tại data center của bạn. Lưu ý: dịch vụ container (ECS, EKS, Fargate) là nhóm riêng biệt, được đề cập trong bài tiếp theo.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Amazon EC2
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) is a computing service that runs virtual servers in the cloud. It is classified as IaaS — the basic building block of cloud infrastructure.

  • Launch Linux or Windows virtual machines ("instances")
  • Choose OS, storage, virtual CPUs (Intel, AMD, or AWS Graviton)
  • Pay-as-you-go or reserve for a specific period
  • Access and manage remotely via browser, CLI, or API
"Elastic" = the instance can be resized, customized, or scaled at any time. Auto Scaling can increase or decrease the number of instances based on traffic.

Shared Responsibility on EC2

AWS managesData centers, hardware, host OS, hypervisor
You manageGuest OS patching, security groups, IAM, your data
sections.contentVi: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) là dịch vụ tính toán chạy máy ảo (virtual server) trên đám mây, được phân loại là IaaS — khối xây dựng cơ bản của hạ tầng đám mây. Bạn có thể: khởi chạy máy ảo Linux hoặc Windows ('instances'), chọn OS, lưu trữ, số CPU ảo (Intel, AMD hoặc AWS Graviton), trả theo sử dụng hoặc đặt trước trong thời gian cụ thể, truy cập và quản lý từ xa qua trình duyệt, CLI hoặc API. 'Elastic' = instance có thể thay đổi kích thước, tùy chỉnh hoặc scale bất cứ lúc nào. Auto Scaling tự động tăng/giảm số instance theo lưu lượng. Shared Responsibility: AWS quản lý data center, phần cứng, Host OS, hypervisor; bạn quản lý Guest OS, security groups, IAM và dữ liệu.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: AWS Lambda
sections.contentHtml:
AWS Lambda is a serverless computing service — run code without managing any servers. You pay only for the compute time you consume.

  • Upload code → Lambda runs it as a Lambda function
  • Instantly scales to thousands of requests per second
  • Supports Java, Go, Ruby, Node.js, Python, and more via runtimes
  • You CANNOT access or manage the underlying servers (unlike EC2)
Serverless ≠ no servers. Servers still exist, but AWS manages them entirely. Lambda is fully managed — you only own the function code.
sections.contentVi: AWS Lambda là dịch vụ serverless — chạy code mà không cần quản lý bất kỳ server nào, chỉ trả tiền cho thời gian tính toán thực sự sử dụng. Cách dùng: upload code → Lambda chạy code đó dưới dạng 'Lambda function'. Tự động scale đến hàng nghìn request mỗi giây. Hỗ trợ Java, Go, Ruby, Node.js, Python và nhiều ngôn ngữ khác qua runtimes. Bạn KHÔNG THỂ truy cập hoặc quản lý các server bên dưới — khác với EC2. Lưu ý quan trọng: Serverless ≠ không có server. Server vẫn tồn tại nhưng AWS quản lý hoàn toàn. Lambda là fully managed — bạn chỉ sở hữu code hàm.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: AWS Batch & Elastic Beanstalk (Orchestration)
sections.contentHtml:
Both services orchestrate EC2 instances — they don't compute themselves but coordinate fleets of EC2 instances.

AWS BatchRuns batch computing workloads. Dynamically provisions the right EC2 quantity/type. No need to install batch software or manage server clusters.
Elastic BeanstalkAutomates app deployment: capacity provisioning, load balancing, DB setup, auto scaling, health monitoring. You just upload your app.
Key difference from Lambda: With Batch and Elastic Beanstalk, you CAN access and manage the underlying EC2 instances. With Lambda, you cannot.
sections.contentVi: Cả hai dịch vụ điều phối EC2 instances — chúng không tự tính toán mà phối hợp đội máy EC2. AWS Batch: chạy batch computing workload, tự động cung cấp đúng số lượng và loại EC2, không cần cài phần mềm batch hoặc quản lý server cluster. Elastic Beanstalk: tự động hóa deploy ứng dụng từ đầu đến cuối — cung cấp capacity, load balancing, setup DB, auto scaling, giám sát sức khỏe; bạn chỉ cần upload ứng dụng. Điểm khác biệt quan trọng so với Lambda: với Batch và Elastic Beanstalk, bạn VẪN CÓ THỂ truy cập và quản lý EC2 instances bên dưới. Với Lambda, bạn không thể.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Amazon LightSail & AWS Outposts
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon LightSailVirtual Private Server (VPS) solution. Deploy VMs for a low, predictable monthly price. Has its own separate console from AWS Management Console. Also supports databases, load balancers, DNS records.
AWS OutpostsHybrid service. A physical server rack (~6.5 ft tall, 2 ft wide, 4 ft deep) delivered to your on-premises data center. Run EC2 and other AWS services locally. AWS delivers it fully assembled — plug in, configure, run.
sections.contentVi: Amazon LightSail là giải pháp Virtual Private Server (VPS) — deploy máy ảo với giá hàng tháng thấp và cố định. Có console riêng tách biệt với AWS Management Console. Cũng hỗ trợ cơ sở dữ liệu, load balancer, DNS records. Phù hợp cho dự án đơn giản không cần toàn bộ tính năng AWS. AWS Outposts là dịch vụ hybrid — rack server vật lý cao ~2m, rộng 60cm, sâu 1.2m được AWS giao đến data center của bạn. Cho phép chạy EC2 và dịch vụ AWS khác ngay tại chỗ (on-premises). AWS giao đến đã lắp ráp sẵn — cắm điện, cấu hình và chạy ngay. Phù hợp cho tổ chức cần giữ một phần hạ tầng on-premises.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Compute Services Comparison
sections.contentHtml:
EC2IaaS · VMs · access underlying server · you patch Guest OS
LambdaServerless · no server access · pay per execution time · fully managed
BeanstalkOrchestration · EC2 under hood · full access retained · auto deploy
BatchOrchestration · EC2 under hood · batch/scheduled workloads
LightSailVPS · fixed monthly price · separate console
OutpostsHybrid · physical rack · run AWS on-premises
sections.contentVi: Bảng so sánh dịch vụ tính toán: EC2 = IaaS, máy ảo, có thể truy cập server, bạn tự vá Guest OS. Lambda = serverless, không thể truy cập server, trả tiền theo thời gian thực thi, fully managed. Elastic Beanstalk = orchestration, có EC2 bên dưới, vẫn truy cập được EC2, tự động deploy. Batch = orchestration, có EC2 bên dưới, xử lý batch/scheduled workload. LightSail = VPS giá cố định hàng tháng, console riêng, dành cho dự án đơn giản. Outposts = hybrid, rack vật lý, chạy AWS tại on-premises.

summary: EC2 = IaaS virtual machines — elastic, customizable, you patch Guest OS; AWS manages Host OS and hypervisor
Lambda = serverless compute — pay per execution time, no server access, AWS fully manages underlying infrastructure
Batch & Elastic Beanstalk = orchestration services that coordinate EC2 fleets; unlike Lambda, you CAN access underlying EC2
LightSail = VPS with predictable monthly pricing and its own separate console
AWS Outposts = physical rack delivered to your on-premises data center to run AWS services locally (hybrid)
summaryVi: EC2 = IaaS máy ảo — elastic, tùy chỉnh được, bạn tự vá Guest OS; AWS quản lý Host OS và hypervisor
Lambda = serverless, trả tiền theo thời gian thực thi, không thể truy cập server, AWS quản lý hoàn toàn hạ tầng
Batch và Elastic Beanstalk = orchestration service điều phối EC2; khác Lambda, bạn VẪN có thể truy cập EC2
LightSail = VPS giá hàng tháng cố định, có console riêng, phù hợp dự án đơn giản
AWS Outposts = rack vật lý giao đến data center của bạn để chạy dịch vụ AWS tại chỗ (hybrid)

quiz.question: A startup needs to run a web application with unpredictable traffic spikes. The engineering team wants full control over the operating system, installed software, and network configuration. They also need to apply custom security patches on their own schedule. Which AWS compute service BEST meets these requirements?
options: AWS Lambda
Amazon EC2
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
Amazon LightSail
quiz.explanation: EC2 is IaaS and gives full control over the Guest OS, installed software, and patching schedule — exactly what the team requires. Lambda is serverless with no OS access. Elastic Beanstalk abstracts away much of the OS management. LightSail offers a simplified VPS but with less granular control than raw EC2.

quiz.question: A company runs a nightly data processing job that transforms CSV files uploaded to S3. The job takes between 30 seconds and 3 minutes to complete, runs only when a file is uploaded, and the team wants to eliminate all server management. Which compute option is MOST cost-effective?
options: A reserved EC2 instance that polls S3 for new files
AWS Lambda triggered by S3 event notifications
An EC2 Auto Scaling group that scales in after each run
AWS Batch running on a dedicated EC2 fleet
quiz.explanation: Lambda is ideal here: it triggers automatically on S3 events, runs for the exact duration needed, and charges only for execution time with no idle costs. A reserved EC2 instance charges 24/7 even when idle. Auto Scaling incurs launch latency and minimum instance costs. Batch adds orchestration overhead unnecessary for a simple short-lived job.

quiz.question: An enterprise needs to process 100,000 genomics analysis jobs per month. Each job runs for 5–60 minutes on a compute-optimized instance. The team needs to queue jobs, manage dependencies between steps, and automatically provision and terminate EC2 instances. Which service provides the LEAST operational overhead for this workload?
options: AWS Lambda with SQS queuing
Amazon EC2 with a custom job scheduler on each instance
AWS Batch
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with worker environments
quiz.explanation: AWS Batch is purpose-built to queue, schedule, and manage fleets of EC2 instances for batch processing jobs. It handles provisioning, job dependencies, and termination automatically. Lambda has a 15-minute execution limit, making it unsuitable for 60-minute jobs. A custom EC2 scheduler requires significant operational work. Elastic Beanstalk worker environments are designed for web-tier workloads, not large-scale batch processing.

quiz.question: A small business owner with no cloud experience wants to host a WordPress website on AWS. They need a predictable monthly bill, a simple management console, and do not require advanced networking features. Which service is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon EC2 with on-demand pricing
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
Amazon LightSail
AWS Lambda with a container image
quiz.explanation: LightSail is designed for simple use cases with predictable flat monthly pricing and its own simplified console — ideal for non-technical users hosting websites like WordPress. EC2 on-demand pricing can be unpredictable and requires more configuration knowledge. Elastic Beanstalk is more complex and geared toward developers deploying applications. Lambda with containers is not suitable for traditional CMS workloads.

quiz.question: A financial services company operates in a highly regulated environment. Due to data residency laws, all compute workloads must run in the company's own data center. However, they want to use native AWS services, APIs, and tooling for consistency with their cloud environment. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Deploy EC2 instances in a private VPC with no internet gateway
Use AWS Local Zones to place compute closer to on-premises
AWS Outposts installed in the company's on-premises data center
Use AWS Direct Connect to run EC2 workloads with low latency from on-premises
quiz.explanation: AWS Outposts delivers physical AWS-managed hardware racks to the customer's on-premises data center, allowing AWS services to run locally while meeting strict data residency requirements. VPC with no internet gateway still runs in AWS regions. Local Zones reduce latency to end-users but still run in AWS-owned facilities. Direct Connect provides connectivity to AWS but does not move compute on-premises.

quiz.question: A team is responsible for a web application deployed using AWS Elastic Beanstalk. A security audit requires the team to log in to the underlying EC2 instances to verify a specific kernel-level configuration. Is this possible, and why?
options: No — Elastic Beanstalk abstracts the infrastructure completely, and underlying EC2 instances cannot be accessed
Yes — Elastic Beanstalk orchestrates EC2 instances, and you can SSH into them or use EC2 Instance Connect
No — only AWS Support can access the underlying instances for managed services
Yes — but only through the AWS Systems Manager Session Manager because SSH is disabled by default on all Beanstalk instances
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is an orchestration service that manages EC2 fleets on your behalf, but those EC2 instances remain accessible to you — you can SSH in or use EC2 Instance Connect. This distinguishes Beanstalk from Lambda, where AWS fully manages the underlying infrastructure and you have no server access. Option D is a partial truth but incorrectly states SSH is disabled by default.

quiz.question: A company's on-premises HPC cluster runs simulations that require low-latency access to a local Oracle database that cannot be migrated to the cloud. The company wants to modernize the simulation orchestration layer using AWS-native tools without moving the database. Which approach has the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Migrate the simulation workload to EC2 and use AWS Direct Connect for database access
Deploy AWS Outposts on-premises so simulation jobs can run with AWS tooling while accessing the local database directly
Use AWS Batch with EC2 instances in a VPC connected to on-premises via VPN
Rewrite simulations as Lambda functions and use RDS Proxy to connect to the on-premises database
quiz.explanation: AWS Outposts places AWS-managed compute on-premises, allowing the simulation workload to run with AWS-native tooling while maintaining direct low-latency access to the local Oracle database. Migrating to EC2 or Batch still routes traffic over the WAN, adding latency. Lambda has a 15-minute timeout unsuitable for long-running HPC jobs, and connecting to an on-premises Oracle DB via RDS Proxy is not a standard pattern.

quiz.question: A company is evaluating AWS compute services for two different workloads: (1) a REST API that handles thousands of short-lived requests per second, and (2) a nightly batch job processing large datasets for 4 hours. The team wants the MOST cost-effective solutions with the LEAST operational overhead. (Choose TWO)
options: Use AWS Lambda for the REST API workload
Use a fleet of on-demand EC2 instances for the REST API workload
Use AWS Batch for the nightly batch job
Use AWS Lambda for the nightly batch job
Use Amazon LightSail for the REST API workload
quiz.explanation: Lambda is ideal for high-volume short-lived REST API requests — it scales automatically, charges per execution, and requires no server management. AWS Batch is ideal for the 4-hour nightly job — it provisions and terminates EC2 capacity automatically around the job schedule. Lambda cannot run 4-hour jobs (15-minute limit). On-demand EC2 for a REST API requires manual scaling management. LightSail lacks the auto-scaling needed for thousands of requests per second.

quiz.question: A developer deploys an application using AWS Elastic Beanstalk and notices a performance issue. They want to tune JVM heap settings directly on the application servers. Which statement is TRUE about this scenario?
options: This is not possible because Elastic Beanstalk fully manages all server configurations
The developer can access the EC2 instances managed by Elastic Beanstalk and modify JVM settings directly or via .ebextensions configuration files
The developer must migrate to EC2 directly to make any OS-level configuration changes
JVM settings can only be changed by submitting a support ticket to AWS
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk orchestrates EC2 instances but does not prevent you from accessing or configuring them. Developers can SSH into instances or use .ebextensions to apply configuration changes, including JVM settings. Elastic Beanstalk reduces operational burden but does not take away access — that is the key difference from Lambda.

quiz.question: An architect is designing a system where the company's security team insists that no OS-level patching responsibility should fall on the application team — AWS must handle all infrastructure patching. The application consists of small event-driven functions that run in under 5 minutes. Which compute model satisfies the security team's requirement?
options: EC2 instances managed by AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with managed platform updates enabled
AWS Lambda
EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group with the latest AMI automatically
quiz.explanation: Lambda is fully managed by AWS — developers have no access to the underlying infrastructure and AWS handles all patching of the host OS and hypervisor. With EC2 (even with Patch Manager or Auto Scaling), the customer is still responsible for patching the Guest OS. Elastic Beanstalk with managed updates patches the platform layer but you remain responsible for the OS if you use custom AMIs or access instances.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is comparing EC2 and Lambda for a new application. Which TWO statements accurately describe the shared responsibility model differences between EC2 and Lambda? (Choose TWO)
options: With EC2, AWS manages the Host OS and hypervisor while the customer manages the Guest OS
With Lambda, the customer must patch the runtime environment and underlying OS
With Lambda, AWS manages all underlying infrastructure including the OS and hypervisor
With EC2, the customer is responsible for the physical security of the hardware
With EC2, AWS manages Guest OS patching by default
quiz.explanation: On EC2, AWS owns the physical host, hypervisor, and Host OS — the customer is responsible for the Guest OS (patching, hardening, etc.). With Lambda, AWS fully manages everything below the function code including runtime, OS, and hypervisor. Customers never patch Lambda infrastructure. AWS always handles physical security regardless of compute model.

quiz.question: A media company runs video transcoding jobs that each take between 20 and 90 minutes. Jobs arrive in bursts — dozens at once followed by hours of inactivity. The team wants automatic scaling, no idle compute costs, and access to the underlying servers for custom codec installation. Which service is MOST appropriate?
options: AWS Lambda with container images
AWS Batch with EC2 compute environments
Amazon LightSail with multiple instance plans
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with worker environments
quiz.explanation: AWS Batch with EC2 compute environments is the best fit: it scales EC2 capacity up for bursts and down to zero during inactivity (no idle costs), handles queuing automatically, and gives access to the underlying EC2 instances where custom codecs can be installed. Lambda cannot run 90-minute jobs. LightSail has no job queue or auto-scaling for burst workloads. Elastic Beanstalk is designed for web apps, not burst batch processing.

quiz.question: A company wants to migrate a legacy on-premises application that uses a local NFS share for inter-process communication. The application cannot be refactored and requires low-latency access to the file share. The company wants to keep using AWS APIs for monitoring and management. Which solution has the LEAST operational overhead while meeting these constraints?
options: Lift and shift the application to EC2 and use Amazon EFS for the NFS share
Deploy AWS Outposts on-premises and run the application locally with AWS management tooling
Use AWS Storage Gateway to expose an NFS interface to EC2 instances in a VPC
Run the application on EC2 and connect to the on-premises NFS over Direct Connect
quiz.explanation: AWS Outposts keeps the application and the NFS share in the same physical location, maintaining ultra-low latency and avoiding refactoring, while providing AWS APIs for monitoring and management. Moving the NFS to EFS on EC2 still creates WAN latency for any on-premises component. Storage Gateway adds complexity and is not designed for inter-process communication. Direct Connect to on-premises NFS from EC2 introduces network latency.

quiz.question: A freelance developer wants to host a personal project — a Node.js API and a PostgreSQL database — with a fixed monthly budget of $20. They have limited AWS experience and want a straightforward setup without configuring VPCs, security groups, or IAM roles in detail. Which service is MOST suitable?
options: Amazon EC2 t3.micro with RDS Free Tier
AWS Lambda with Amazon Aurora Serverless
Amazon LightSail
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with a single-instance environment
quiz.explanation: LightSail bundles compute, storage, and networking into predictable flat monthly pricing plans starting well under $20, and its simplified console does not require deep VPC or IAM knowledge. EC2 with RDS involves multiple services to configure and unpredictable billing. Lambda with Aurora Serverless can be cost-effective but requires understanding of serverless patterns and cold starts. Elastic Beanstalk, while managed, still exposes significant AWS configuration complexity.

quiz.question: A company is deploying a new application and must choose between AWS Elastic Beanstalk and AWS Lambda. Which TWO characteristics apply to Elastic Beanstalk but NOT to Lambda? (Choose TWO)
options: You can SSH into the underlying compute instances
AWS manages all patching of the underlying host OS and hypervisor
You can install custom software on the underlying operating system
The service charges only for the milliseconds your code executes
No maximum execution duration limit applies to individual jobs
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk manages EC2 instances that remain accessible — you can SSH in and install custom software, custom agents, or OS-level tools. Lambda provides no server access whatsoever. Both services have AWS managing host-level infrastructure, but Lambda additionally manages the runtime — customers cannot customize the OS. Lambda charges per execution millisecond; Beanstalk charges for underlying EC2/RDS resources continuously.

quiz.question: A company's operations team receives alerts at 2 AM whenever their on-premises servers experience hardware failures. They are migrating to AWS and the CTO asks: 'Who will be responsible for replacing faulty hardware if we move our workloads to EC2?' What is the CORRECT answer?
options: The company's operations team, using AWS hardware replacement tools in the console
AWS is responsible for all physical hardware maintenance and replacement
A shared responsibility: AWS replaces hardware but the company must initiate a ticket
AWS replaces hardware only for Dedicated Hosts; for standard EC2, the company is responsible
quiz.explanation: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, AWS is fully responsible for the physical infrastructure including hardware maintenance, replacement, and data center operations. Customers are never responsible for physical hardware on EC2, regardless of tenancy type (shared, dedicated instance, or Dedicated Host). This is one of the fundamental benefits of cloud IaaS.

quiz.question: An architect needs to choose between deploying a web application on EC2 directly versus using Elastic Beanstalk. The team wants automatic environment provisioning, health monitoring, and rolling deployments, but also needs the ability to apply a custom SELinux policy to each server. Which choice and reasoning is CORRECT?
options: Use EC2 directly — Elastic Beanstalk does not support custom OS-level security policies
Use Elastic Beanstalk — it supports custom OS configuration via .ebextensions and provides access to underlying EC2 instances where SELinux policies can be applied
Use Lambda — it supports OS-level policy injection at the container level
Use LightSail — it offers the same orchestration as Elastic Beanstalk with simpler configuration management
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk provides orchestration features (provisioning, health monitoring, rolling deployments) while still allowing access to underlying EC2 instances. Custom OS policies like SELinux can be applied via .ebextensions configuration files or direct SSH. Lambda does not provide OS-level access. LightSail lacks enterprise orchestration features.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is documenting which AWS compute services allow direct access to the underlying EC2 instances. (Choose THREE services where this is possible.)
options: AWS Lambda
Amazon EC2
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
AWS Batch (with EC2 compute environment)
Amazon LightSail
quiz.explanation: EC2, Elastic Beanstalk, and AWS Batch (with EC2 compute environments) all run on EC2 instances that the customer can access directly via SSH or SSM. LightSail also runs on virtual servers you can access, but the question specifically targets EC2-backed services; however, the key insight from the lesson is that Lambda is the only service where you have NO access to underlying infrastructure — AWS fully manages it.

quiz.question: A company wants to extend its on-premises VMware environment with AWS services to enable hybrid cloud operations. They need to run EC2 instances and S3 APIs locally within their data center. Which AWS service should they use?
options: AWS Local Zones
AWS Wavelength
AWS Outposts
AWS Direct Connect with a Transit Gateway
quiz.explanation: AWS Outposts is specifically designed to run AWS infrastructure on-premises, including EC2 instances and S3 on-premises APIs, enabling true hybrid operations from the customer's own data center. Local Zones and Wavelength extend AWS infrastructure to metro areas or telecom networks but are still AWS-owned facilities. Direct Connect provides connectivity to AWS regions but does not run AWS compute in the customer's data center.

quiz.question: A company is running a critical application on AWS Elastic Beanstalk. During a security review, the auditor asks: 'Is AWS or the customer responsible for patching the operating system on the application servers?' What is the CORRECT response?
options: AWS is fully responsible for all OS patching because Elastic Beanstalk is a managed service
The customer is responsible for patching the Guest OS on the underlying EC2 instances, even though Elastic Beanstalk handles platform-level managed updates
Responsibility is split: AWS patches the OS during business hours, and the customer patches after hours
The customer has no access to the OS, so neither party patches it — it is patched automatically by Amazon Linux
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is an orchestration layer over EC2, not a fully managed service like Lambda. The customer retains responsibility for the Guest OS on the underlying EC2 instances. While Elastic Beanstalk's managed platform updates can handle platform runtime upgrades, OS-level patching remains the customer's responsibility. This contrasts with Lambda, where AWS manages all infrastructure including the OS.

lessons.title: AWS Container Services
lessons.overview: Learn AWS container services: ECS for Docker orchestration, EKS for Kubernetes (cloud-agnostic), Fargate for serverless containers, ECR for image storage, and migration tools App2Container and Copilot.
lessons.overviewVi: Tìm hiểu các dịch vụ container AWS: ECS cho Docker orchestration, EKS cho Kubernetes (cloud-agnostic), Fargate cho serverless container, ECR để lưu images, và các công cụ migration App2Container và Copilot.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: Container vs Virtual Machine
sections.contentHtml:
Virtual MachineEmulates an entire computer including a full Guest OS. Requires a hypervisor. Heavier, more resource-intensive.
ContainerEmulates only the application and its dependencies — not the full OS. Lightweight. Uses Docker or Kubernetes.
Containers are ideal for microservices architecture — package an application with its runtime dependencies into a portable, isolated unit.
sections.contentVi: Máy ảo mô phỏng toàn bộ máy tính bao gồm cả Guest OS đầy đủ — cần hypervisor, nặng và tốn nhiều tài nguyên hơn. Container chỉ mô phỏng ứng dụng và dependencies của nó — không cần Guest OS riêng, nhẹ hơn nhiều, dùng Docker hoặc Kubernetes. Container lý tưởng cho microservices architecture — đóng gói ứng dụng cùng runtime dependencies thành một đơn vị portable và cô lập.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon ECS is a highly scalable container orchestration service for Docker containers. It manages the cluster of servers that power your containers.

  • Containers are defined in a task definition — used to run individual tasks or grouped as a service
  • Runs on AWS Fargate (serverless) or Amazon EC2 (you manage)
  • Specify an IAM role for containers in an ECS task
  • Integrates with EFS/FSx (storage), SQS/Kinesis (messaging), ECS Auto Scaling
ECS uses Docker, which means it is not cloud-agnostic — it's tightly coupled with the AWS ecosystem.
sections.contentVi: Amazon ECS là dịch vụ orchestration container có khả năng mở rộng cao dành cho Docker containers. Nó quản lý cluster các server chạy containers. Containers được định nghĩa trong task definition — dùng để chạy tasks đơn lẻ hoặc nhóm thành service. Có thể chạy trên AWS Fargate (serverless) hoặc Amazon EC2 (bạn quản lý). Bạn chỉ định IAM role cho containers trong ECS task. Tích hợp với EFS/FSx (lưu trữ), SQS/Kinesis (messaging), ECS Auto Scaling. ECS dùng Docker nên không cloud-agnostic — gắn chặt với hệ sinh thái AWS.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon EKS is a fully managed Kubernetes service. Kubernetes is an open-source platform for managing containerized workloads.

  • Containers are grouped into pods — the basic operational unit in Kubernetes
  • Pods can run on AWS Fargate or Amazon EC2
  • Kubernetes is cloud-agnostic — containers can migrate to Azure, GCP, or on-premises
ECS vs EKS: ECS uses Docker (AWS-native), EKS uses Kubernetes (portable). If you need to move containers to other clouds, use EKS/Kubernetes.
sections.contentVi: Amazon EKS là dịch vụ Kubernetes được quản lý hoàn toàn. Kubernetes là nền tảng open-source để quản lý containerized workloads. Containers được nhóm thành pods — đơn vị vận hành cơ bản trong Kubernetes. Pods có thể chạy trên AWS Fargate hoặc Amazon EC2. Kubernetes là cloud-agnostic — containers có thể di chuyển sang Azure, GCP hoặc on-premises dễ dàng. So sánh ECS và EKS: ECS dùng Docker (native AWS), EKS dùng Kubernetes (portable). Nếu cần chuyển containers sang cloud khác, hãy dùng EKS/Kubernetes.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: AWS Fargate
sections.contentHtml:
AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers. It works with both Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS.

  • No server provisioning, scaling, or management needed
  • Pay only for the resources required to run your containers
  • Each ECS task or Kubernetes pod runs in its own isolated kernel
  • Avoids over-provisioning — no idle EC2 instances to pay for
Fargate is to containers what Lambda is to functions — serverless, fully managed, no underlying server access.
sections.contentVi: AWS Fargate là serverless compute engine cho containers, hoạt động với cả Amazon ECS và Amazon EKS. Không cần cung cấp, scale hoặc quản lý server. Chỉ trả tiền cho tài nguyên cần thiết để chạy containers. Mỗi ECS task hoặc Kubernetes pod chạy trong kernel cô lập riêng — cô lập tốt hơn so với chia sẻ EC2 host. Tránh over-provisioning — không có EC2 nhàn rỗi phải trả tiền. Fargate với container = Lambda với function — serverless, fully managed, không thể truy cập server bên dưới.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: ECR, App2Container & Copilot
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon ECRFully managed Docker container registry — store, manage, and deploy Docker images. Integrated with ECS. IAM provides resource-level control per repository.
AWS App2Container (A2C)CLI tool to modernize .NET and Java apps into containers. Analyzes running apps, packages artifacts + dependencies into Docker images, generates ECS task / Kubernetes pod definitions.
AWS CopilotCLI to quickly launch and manage containerized applications on AWS. Automates entire deployment lifecycle: task definition → cluster → image push to registry.
sections.contentVi: Amazon ECR là registry Docker container được quản lý hoàn toàn — lưu trữ, quản lý và deploy Docker images. Tích hợp với ECS. IAM cung cấp kiểm soát cấp resource theo từng repository. AWS App2Container (A2C) là công cụ CLI để hiện đại hóa ứng dụng .NET và Java thành container. A2C phân tích ứng dụng đang chạy, đóng gói artifacts và dependencies thành Docker images, tạo ECS task definition hoặc Kubernetes pod definitions. AWS Copilot là CLI để nhanh chóng khởi chạy và quản lý containerized applications trên AWS — tự động hóa toàn bộ deployment lifecycle: từ task definition đến cluster đến push image lên registry.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Container Service Comparison
sections.contentHtml:
ECSDocker · AWS-native · task definition · Fargate or EC2
EKSKubernetes · cloud-agnostic · pods · Fargate or EC2
FargateServerless containers · works with ECS & EKS · isolated kernel per task
ECRDocker image registry · integrated with ECS · IAM per repo
  • Need cloud-agnostic containers? → EKS (Kubernetes)
  • Need serverless containers? → Fargate
  • Need to store Docker images? → ECR
  • Migrating .NET/Java to containers? → App2Container
sections.contentVi: Cloud-agnostic containers → EKS (Kubernetes). Serverless containers → Fargate. Lưu Docker images → ECR. Di chuyển .NET/Java sang container → App2Container. ECS và EKS đều có thể dùng Fargate (serverless) hoặc EC2 (bạn quản lý) làm compute engine. Câu hỏi thi: cần di chuyển sang cloud khác → EKS; cần serverless → Fargate; cần chạy Docker native AWS → ECS.

summary: Container = lightweight application emulation (no full Guest OS); VM = full computer emulation. Containers are ideal for microservices.
ECS = Docker orchestration (AWS-native). EKS = Kubernetes (cloud-agnostic, portable to other clouds). Both support Fargate or EC2.
Fargate = serverless compute for containers (ECS + EKS); no server management, isolated kernel per task, pay per use
ECR = Docker container registry. App2Container = migrate .NET/Java to containers. Copilot = CLI for full container deployment lifecycle.
summaryVi: Container = mô phỏng ứng dụng nhẹ (không cần Guest OS đầy đủ); VM = mô phỏng toàn bộ máy tính. Container lý tưởng cho microservices.
ECS = Docker orchestration (AWS-native). EKS = Kubernetes (cloud-agnostic, di chuyển sang cloud khác được). Cả hai hỗ trợ Fargate hoặc EC2.
Fargate = serverless compute cho containers (ECS + EKS); không quản lý server, kernel cô lập mỗi task, trả theo sử dụng
ECR = Docker container registry. App2Container = di chuyển .NET/Java sang container. Copilot = CLI cho toàn bộ deployment lifecycle.

quiz.question: A startup is building a microservices application where each service needs to be deployed independently and scaled separately. The team wants the LEAST operational overhead for managing the underlying infrastructure. Which solution should a solutions architect recommend?
options: Deploy each microservice on a separate Amazon EC2 instance and use Auto Scaling groups
Use Amazon ECS with AWS Fargate to run containers without managing servers
Use Amazon EKS with self-managed EC2 worker nodes for maximum control
Package each microservice as an AMI and deploy using AWS Elastic Beanstalk
quiz.explanation: AWS Fargate with ECS provides serverless container execution, eliminating the need to provision or manage servers — the lowest operational overhead option. EC2-based approaches require managing instances, while EKS with self-managed nodes adds Kubernetes operational complexity. Elastic Beanstalk is not designed for multi-service container orchestration at this level.

quiz.question: A company currently runs a .NET monolithic application on on-premises servers and wants to modernize it by migrating to containers on AWS. The team has limited container expertise. Which AWS service is specifically designed to help with this migration?
options: AWS Copilot
Amazon ECR
AWS App2Container
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
quiz.explanation: AWS App2Container is specifically designed to containerize existing .NET and Java applications running on-premises or on EC2, automating the discovery, analysis, and containerization process. Copilot helps deploy containerized apps but does not migrate legacy apps to containers. ECR is a container registry. Elastic Beanstalk does not migrate legacy .NET apps to containers.

quiz.question: A financial services company runs containerized workloads on Amazon ECS. Security requirements mandate that each container task must run with an isolated kernel and cannot share underlying OS resources with other tasks. Which launch type satisfies this requirement?
options: ECS on EC2 with dedicated instances
ECS on EC2 with placement constraints
ECS with AWS Fargate
ECS on EC2 with security groups isolating each task
quiz.explanation: AWS Fargate provides kernel-level isolation per task — each task gets its own isolated kernel, making it suitable for high-security workloads. ECS on EC2, even with dedicated instances or placement constraints, shares the host OS kernel across tasks running on the same instance. Security groups control network traffic but do not provide kernel isolation.

quiz.question: A company is adopting containers and plans to eventually run workloads across multiple cloud providers to avoid vendor lock-in. They need a container orchestration platform that is portable across clouds. Which AWS service best meets this requirement?
options: Amazon ECS with Fargate
Amazon EKS
AWS Copilot
Amazon ECS with EC2 launch type
quiz.explanation: Amazon EKS runs Kubernetes, which is a cloud-agnostic, open-source orchestration platform that can run on any cloud provider, making it the most portable choice. ECS is an AWS-native service with no direct equivalent on other clouds. Copilot is a CLI tool for ECS/App Runner deployment, not a portable orchestration platform.

quiz.question: A development team stores Docker container images and needs a secure, managed registry that integrates natively with Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS. Which service should they use?
options: AWS CodeArtifact
Amazon S3 with versioning enabled
Amazon ECR
AWS CodeCommit
quiz.explanation: Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) is the fully managed Docker container image registry that integrates natively with ECS and EKS for pulling images. CodeArtifact is a package manager for code artifacts (npm, Maven, etc.), not container images. S3 can store files but is not a container registry. CodeCommit is a Git repository service.

quiz.question: A company runs 50 containerized microservices on Amazon ECS. Some services run continuously, while others spike unpredictably. The team wants the MOST cost-effective approach that scales automatically without pre-provisioning capacity.
options: Run all services on ECS with EC2 launch type and Reserved Instances for predictable workloads, On-Demand for unpredictable ones
Run all services on ECS with AWS Fargate so you pay only for the vCPU and memory consumed per task
Run all services on EC2 Spot Instances managed by ECS to minimize cost
Run all services on ECS with EC2 Auto Scaling groups using Savings Plans
quiz.explanation: Fargate charges only for the vCPU and memory resources actually consumed while a task is running — ideal for variable workloads requiring no capacity pre-provisioning. Spot Instances can be interrupted, which is risky for continuous services. EC2-based approaches require managing and pre-provisioning clusters even when idle, adding cost and overhead.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is designing a containerized microservices platform on AWS. The company needs Docker image storage and an orchestration layer that supports both serverless and EC2-based compute. (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon ECR for storing and managing Docker container images
AWS CodeDeploy for container image versioning and storage
Amazon ECS to orchestrate containers using either Fargate or EC2 launch types
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling as the primary container orchestration layer
AWS Lambda to orchestrate ECS tasks across availability zones
quiz.explanation: Amazon ECR is the managed container registry for storing Docker images, and Amazon ECS is the orchestration service that supports both Fargate (serverless) and EC2 launch types. CodeDeploy handles application deployments but is not a container registry. EC2 Auto Scaling provides scaling for EC2 instances but is not a container orchestration platform. Lambda orchestrates functions, not ECS tasks at the platform level.

quiz.question: A team of developers wants to deploy a containerized application to AWS with a full CI/CD pipeline — from building the image to deploying to ECS — using a single CLI tool with minimal configuration. Which AWS service is designed for this use case?
options: AWS CloudFormation with ECS resources
AWS Copilot CLI
AWS CDK with ECS constructs
AWS Systems Manager Run Command
quiz.explanation: AWS Copilot is a CLI tool specifically designed to manage the full container application deployment lifecycle on ECS and App Runner, from initial setup through CI/CD pipeline creation, with minimal configuration. CloudFormation and CDK are infrastructure-as-code tools that require more configuration and do not focus exclusively on the container deployment lifecycle. Systems Manager Run Command is for remote command execution on EC2.

quiz.question: A company needs to run containers on AWS. Their operations team is experienced with Kubernetes and has existing Helm charts and Kubernetes manifests from on-premises deployments. Which AWS service allows them to reuse these assets with the LEAST rework?
options: Amazon ECS with Fargate, using task definitions translated from Kubernetes manifests
AWS App2Container to convert Kubernetes manifests to ECS task definitions
Amazon EKS, which runs standard Kubernetes and is compatible with existing Helm charts and manifests
AWS Elastic Beanstalk with Docker platform, importing existing Kubernetes configs
quiz.explanation: Amazon EKS runs upstream Kubernetes, making it fully compatible with existing Helm charts, manifests, and Kubernetes tooling — the least rework needed. ECS uses a different orchestration model (task definitions, services) that is not compatible with Kubernetes manifests directly. App2Container migrates legacy apps to containers, not Kubernetes manifests to ECS. Elastic Beanstalk does not support Kubernetes configurations.

quiz.question: A company wants to run containers on AWS and is comparing ECS with EC2 launch type versus ECS with Fargate. Which characteristics apply to AWS Fargate? (Choose TWO)
options: You must provision and manage EC2 instances in the cluster
Each task runs with an isolated kernel, improving security boundaries
You are billed for EC2 instance uptime regardless of task utilization
No server management is required — AWS manages the underlying compute
Fargate requires you to choose an EC2 instance type for each task
quiz.explanation: Fargate provides isolated kernel per task for enhanced security and eliminates server management — AWS handles the underlying compute infrastructure. With Fargate, you do not manage EC2 instances, you are not billed for idle EC2 capacity, and you do not select instance types (you specify vCPU and memory per task instead).

quiz.question: A media company runs batch video transcoding jobs in containers. Jobs are submitted sporadically and can tolerate interruption. The company wants the MOST cost-effective compute option for these ECS tasks.
options: ECS with Fargate launch type using Fargate Spot
ECS with EC2 launch type using On-Demand Reserved Instances
ECS with Fargate standard pricing with provisioned concurrency
ECS with EC2 launch type using Dedicated Hosts
quiz.explanation: Fargate Spot uses spare AWS capacity at significant discounts (up to 70%) and is ideal for fault-tolerant, interruptible batch workloads like video transcoding. Reserved Instances and Dedicated Hosts require upfront commitment and are not cost-effective for sporadic workloads. Standard Fargate pricing without Spot does not offer the same cost savings for interruptible jobs.

quiz.question: A company is evaluating containers versus virtual machines for a new microservices architecture. Which statement correctly differentiates containers from VMs in the context of AWS deployments?
options: Containers emulate full hardware including a complete Guest OS, while VMs share the host OS kernel
Containers share the host OS kernel and are lightweight, while VMs emulate a full computer including a Guest OS
Containers require more memory than VMs because they include a full operating system per service
VMs start faster than containers because they use pre-built AMIs with cached OS images
quiz.explanation: Containers are lightweight because they share the host OS kernel and do not bundle a full Guest OS — only the application and its dependencies. VMs emulate a complete computer including a full Guest OS per instance. This makes containers faster to start and more resource-efficient, which is why they are ideal for microservices. VMs generally start slower due to full OS boot.

quiz.question: An e-commerce company runs critical containerized services on Amazon EKS. They want to ensure the platform remains highly available across failures and also retain the ability to migrate to another cloud in the future without re-architecting. Which configuration provides the HIGHEST availability while preserving portability?
options: Amazon ECS with Fargate across multiple Availability Zones
Amazon EKS with managed node groups across multiple Availability Zones
Amazon ECS with EC2 Auto Scaling across multiple Availability Zones
Amazon EKS with Fargate profiles across multiple Availability Zones
quiz.explanation: EKS with Fargate across multiple AZs provides both high availability (multi-AZ resilience) and cloud portability (Kubernetes is cloud-agnostic). ECS-based options do not offer the same portability since ECS is AWS-native. EKS with managed node groups is highly available but requires more server management than Fargate. The Fargate option eliminates node management while retaining Kubernetes portability.

quiz.question: A company wants to containerize a legacy Java application running on an on-premises server and deploy it to Amazon ECS. Which TWO AWS services should they use to accomplish this migration and deployment? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS App2Container to analyze and containerize the legacy Java application
AWS Database Migration Service to convert the Java app to a container image
Amazon ECR to store the resulting Docker container image
AWS CodeCommit to store the Java source code as a container artifact
Amazon CloudWatch to convert application logs into a container definition
quiz.explanation: App2Container analyzes and containerizes .NET and Java applications, producing a Docker image and ECS task definition. Amazon ECR then stores that Docker image securely for ECS to pull during deployment. DMS is for database migrations, not application containerization. CodeCommit stores source code, not container images. CloudWatch is a monitoring service, not a migration tool.

quiz.question: A company's development team is deploying a new containerized web application to Amazon ECS for the first time. They want a high-level CLI tool that handles environment setup, load balancer creation, ECR image pushing, and ECS service deployment in a single workflow. Which tool provides this?
options: AWS CLI with ECS subcommands
AWS CloudFormation with nested stacks
AWS Copilot
Amazon ECS Console wizard
quiz.explanation: AWS Copilot is purpose-built to manage the full container application lifecycle — it provisions infrastructure (load balancers, VPCs, ECR repos), builds and pushes images, and deploys ECS services, all through a simplified CLI workflow. The AWS CLI requires individual commands for each step. CloudFormation provides IaC but requires writing templates manually. The ECS Console wizard handles basic setup but not the full CI/CD lifecycle.

quiz.question: A company runs both Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS workloads and wants to use a single serverless compute engine for containers across both platforms to avoid managing EC2 instances. Which service fulfills this requirement?
options: AWS Lambda, which can run containers on both ECS and EKS
AWS Fargate, which provides serverless compute for both ECS and EKS
Amazon EC2 Spot Instances, which automatically manage themselves
AWS Batch, which manages compute for both ECS and EKS workloads
quiz.explanation: AWS Fargate is the serverless compute engine that works with both Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS, removing the need to manage EC2 instances for either platform. Lambda runs functions, not persistent container services, and does not integrate as a compute layer for ECS or EKS. EC2 Spot Instances still require management. AWS Batch is for batch processing and does not serve as the compute layer for EKS.

quiz.question: A SaaS company is designing a multi-tenant containerized platform. Security auditors require that each tenant's containers must not share any OS-level resources, including the kernel, with other tenants. Which deployment model satisfies this requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: ECS on EC2 with one EC2 instance per tenant
EKS with one managed node group per tenant
ECS with Fargate, which provides per-task kernel isolation
ECS on EC2 with Linux cgroups and namespaces configured per tenant
quiz.explanation: Fargate provides kernel-level isolation for each task, meaning tenant containers never share kernel resources — satisfying strict multi-tenant security requirements. ECS/EKS on EC2 with one instance per tenant provides isolation but requires managing EC2 instances (higher operational overhead). Manually configuring cgroups and namespaces on EC2 is complex and operationally heavy, and still shares the host kernel.

quiz.question: A company is choosing between Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS for their container strategy. Which statements are TRUE about these services? (Choose THREE)
options: EKS uses Kubernetes as its orchestration engine, making workloads portable to other Kubernetes-compatible platforms
ECS is a Kubernetes-based service managed by AWS
Both ECS and EKS support AWS Fargate as a serverless compute option
ECS is an AWS-native container orchestration service using its own control plane
EKS requires you to use EC2 launch type and cannot use Fargate
quiz.explanation: EKS runs Kubernetes and is portable to other clouds; both ECS and EKS support Fargate; and ECS is AWS-native with its own orchestration model (not Kubernetes). ECS does NOT use Kubernetes — it uses AWS's own control plane. EKS fully supports Fargate through Fargate profiles, so the claim that EKS cannot use Fargate is incorrect.

quiz.question: A company wants to containerize a microservices application. The architect notes that each microservice currently runs as a separate process and needs independent deployment and scaling. The team wants to avoid the overhead of a full operating system per service. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Deploy each microservice as a separate EC2 instance with a full OS stack
Deploy all microservices into a single large EC2 instance to share resources
Use containers, where each microservice runs in its own container sharing the host OS kernel
Use VMware virtual machines on AWS to isolate each microservice with its own Guest OS
quiz.explanation: Containers are ideal for microservices because each service runs in its own container with isolated dependencies but shares the host OS kernel — lightweight, fast to start, and independently deployable. Separate EC2 instances or VMs per microservice require a full Guest OS per service, wasting resources. A single large EC2 instance eliminates independent scaling and introduces blast radius risk.

quiz.question: A company already uses Amazon ECS to run containerized workloads. They are now expanding to a hybrid model and want container workloads that can run on AWS, on-premises data centers, and potentially Google Cloud in the future — all under the same orchestration platform. What is the MOST suitable service to transition to?
options: Continue with Amazon ECS and use ECS Anywhere for on-premises, accepting the AWS-only cloud limitation
Migrate to Amazon EKS, which uses Kubernetes — a cloud-agnostic platform compatible with on-premises and other clouds
Use AWS Fargate across all environments since Fargate is available in all cloud providers
Use AWS Copilot to manage ECS deployments across multiple clouds
quiz.explanation: Amazon EKS uses Kubernetes, which is cloud-agnostic and runs on AWS, on-premises (via EKS Anywhere or self-managed), and other cloud providers — making it the best choice for a multi-cloud strategy. ECS Anywhere extends ECS to on-premises but ECS itself is AWS-native and not portable to GCP or other clouds. Fargate is an AWS-only service and is not available on other cloud providers. Copilot is a CLI for ECS/App Runner and does not provide multi-cloud orchestration.

lessons.title: AWS Storage Services
lessons.overview: Master all AWS storage categories: block storage (EC2 Instance Store, EBS), object storage (S3, S3 Glacier), shared file storage (EFS, FSx), backup (AWS Backup), and hybrid integration (Storage Gateway).
lessons.overviewVi: Nắm vững tất cả loại lưu trữ AWS: block storage (EC2 Instance Store, EBS), object storage (S3, S3 Glacier), shared file storage (EFS, FSx), backup (AWS Backup) và hybrid integration (Storage Gateway).

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: Storage Types at a Glance
sections.contentHtml:
Block StorageEC2 Instance Store (ephemeral) · Amazon EBS (persistent)
Object StorageAmazon S3 · Amazon S3 Glacier (archival)
File StorageAmazon EFS (Linux/NFS) · FSx for Lustre (HPC/Linux) · FSx for Windows (SMB)
BackupAWS Backup (centralized, automated)
HybridAWS Storage Gateway (connects on-premises to S3)
sections.contentVi: Tổng quan các loại lưu trữ AWS: Block Storage — EC2 Instance Store (tạm thời, mất khi dừng) và Amazon EBS (persistent, tồn tại sau stop). Object Storage — Amazon S3 (không giới hạn) và S3 Glacier (archive). File Storage — Amazon EFS (Linux/NFS, chia sẻ giữa nhiều EC2 khác AZ), FSx for Lustre (HPC Linux hiệu năng cao), FSx for Windows (SMB cho Windows). Backup — AWS Backup (tập trung, tự động). Hybrid — AWS Storage Gateway (kết nối on-premises với S3).
