DMS can directly migrate Oracle to Redshift without any schema conversion
quiz.explanation: AWS DMS supports heterogeneous migrations including Oracle as a source and Redshift as a target. For cross-engine migrations, the AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) is used to convert the schema before DMS handles the data migration. DMS does not perform schema conversion on its own for cross-engine migrations. Saying DMS only supports same-engine is incorrect — cross-engine is a core DMS use case.

quiz.question: A manufacturing company has edge locations with intermittent connectivity and needs to collect IoT data at the edge and transfer it to AWS periodically. Which Snow Family devices could serve this use case? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Snowcone
AWS Snowmobile
AWS Snowball Edge with compute capabilities
AWS DataSync Edge appliance
AWS Storage Gateway hardware appliance
quiz.explanation: Both Snowcone and Snowball Edge support edge computing and local data collection, making them suitable for IoT data collection at locations with intermittent connectivity. Data is collected locally and shipped to AWS for ingestion. Snowmobile is a massive truck for exabyte-scale datacenter migrations, completely unsuitable for edge IoT use cases. There is no DataSync Edge appliance product.

quiz.question: A company's compliance team requires that all file transfers to Amazon S3 from external auditors be encrypted in transit using TLS, but the auditors cannot use SFTP clients — they only have legacy FTP tools. Which AWS Transfer Family protocol should be configured?
options: FTP (plain FTP without encryption)
SFTP (SSH-based encryption)
FTPS (FTP with TLS encryption)
HTTPS with presigned S3 URLs
quiz.explanation: FTPS (FTP over TLS) provides FTP-compatible access with TLS encryption in transit, satisfying the compliance requirement. Auditors can use their existing FTP tools configured for FTPS. Plain FTP has no encryption and does not meet the compliance requirement. SFTP uses SSH rather than TLS and requires SFTP-capable clients, which the auditors lack.

quiz.question: A solutions architect needs to recommend the MOST cost-effective solution for migrating 500 TB of cold archival data from an on-premises storage array to Amazon S3 Glacier. The company has a 200 Mbps internet connection and wants the migration completed within 30 days.
options: AWS DataSync over the existing internet connection
AWS Transfer Family with multipart SFTP uploads
AWS Snowball Edge devices shipped to the data center
AWS Direct Connect with a 10 Gbps dedicated line
quiz.explanation: At 200 Mbps, transferring 500 TB would take approximately 231 days — far exceeding the 30-day requirement. Snowball Edge can handle petabyte-scale offline transfers and complete within the deadline using physical shipment. While Direct Connect at 10 Gbps could transfer 500 TB in about 5 days, provisioning a new Direct Connect circuit takes weeks and is expensive — Snowball is more cost-effective for a one-time migration.

quiz.question: An organization is simultaneously migrating VMs using Server Migration Service, databases using DMS, and files using DataSync. The CTO asks for a unified progress report across all migration streams. Which AWS service provides this consolidated view with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Build a custom CloudWatch dashboard aggregating metrics from all three services
AWS Migration Hub
AWS Config with migration compliance rules
AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter with migration runbooks
quiz.explanation: AWS Migration Hub is specifically designed to provide a single consolidated dashboard for tracking migrations across multiple AWS migration services including SMS, DMS, and DataSync. It requires minimal configuration to aggregate progress. Building a custom CloudWatch dashboard would require significant engineering effort. Config and Systems Manager are operational tools, not migration-tracking platforms.

quiz.question: A company is selecting an offline data transfer method for a one-time migration of approximately 10 PB of data to Amazon S3. Network transfer is not feasible. Which Snow Family options could support this migration? (Choose TWO)
options: Multiple AWS Snowball Edge devices used in parallel
A single AWS Snowcone device
AWS Snowmobile
AWS DataSync in offline mode
A single AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized device
quiz.explanation: At 10 PB scale, both multiple Snowball Edge devices (each handling up to ~80 TB) used in parallel and a single Snowmobile (handling up to 100 PB) are valid options. A single Snowcone is only 8 TB — far too small. A single Snowball Edge cannot handle 10 PB alone. There is no DataSync offline mode; DataSync is an online service requiring network connectivity.

quiz.question: A company runs batch analytics jobs that process files uploaded to S3 by external partners. Partners have been complaining about complex authentication when uploading via the AWS console. The solution architect wants to let partners continue using their familiar file transfer clients with username/password authentication, while routing traffic through the company's domain. Which combination of services achieves this with LEAST operational overhead?
options: EC2-based FTP server + Elastic IP + Route 53 alias record
AWS Transfer Family (SFTP endpoint) + Amazon Route 53 custom domain
S3 presigned URLs + CloudFront distribution with custom domain
AWS DataSync agent + Route 53 health checks
quiz.explanation: AWS Transfer Family provides a fully managed SFTP service with username/password authentication backed by S3, and Route 53 enables custom domain routing so partners connect via the company's branded domain. This eliminates server management overhead. An EC2-based FTP server requires patching and scaling management. S3 presigned URLs require partners to change their workflow from file-transfer clients to HTTPS. DataSync is for migration tasks, not partner-facing file transfer.

lessons.title: AWS Analytics Services
lessons.overview: Discover how AWS analytics services power big data processing, real-time streaming, data warehousing, and ETL workflows. Key services: Kinesis (real-time streaming), Redshift (data warehouse), Glue (serverless ETL), and Athena (serverless SQL on S3).
lessons.overviewVi: Khám phá cách dịch vụ analytics AWS hỗ trợ xử lý big data, streaming thời gian thực, data warehouse và ETL. Các dịch vụ chính: Kinesis (streaming thời gian thực), Redshift (data warehouse), Glue (ETL serverless) và Athena (SQL serverless trên S3).

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: Data Lakes, ETL, and Real-Time Analytics
sections.contentHtml:
AWS provides a comprehensive analytics suite. Key foundational concepts:

Data lakeA centralized repository to store all structured and unstructured data at any scale without pre-processing
Unstructured dataData stored as-is without parsing before processing (raw logs, images, videos)
ETLExtract, Transform, Load — collect data from sources, transform it, load it into a destination (e.g., data warehouse)

Many AWS analytics services are built on open-source tools: Apache Kafka, Spark, Hadoop, Flink, Hive, Lucene, and many more.
sections.contentVi: AWS cung cấp bộ analytics toàn diện. Các khái niệm nền tảng: Data lake — kho lưu trữ tập trung cho tất cả dữ liệu có cấu trúc và phi cấu trúc ở mọi quy mô, không cần xử lý trước; Unstructured data — dữ liệu lưu trữ nguyên trạng không cần phân tích trước (log thô, hình ảnh, video); ETL — Extract, Transform, Load, thu thập dữ liệu từ nguồn, biến đổi và nạp vào đích (ví dụ data warehouse). Nhiều dịch vụ analytics AWS xây dựng trên công cụ open-source: Apache Kafka, Spark, Hadoop, Flink, Hive, Lucene và nhiều hơn nữa.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Amazon Kinesis — Real-Time Streaming
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon Kinesis is a suite that lets you analyze data streams in real time — data available in milliseconds. A data stream is a continuous flow of data (e.g., clickstream from high-traffic websites, IoT sensor readings).

Kinesis Data StreamsCollect and process large streams of data records in real time
Kinesis Data FirehoseLoad streaming data into AWS data stores (S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch) — near real-time
Kinesis Data AnalyticsAnalyze streaming data using SQL or Apache Flink in real time
sections.contentVi: Amazon Kinesis là bộ dịch vụ cho phép phân tích luồng dữ liệu thời gian thực — dữ liệu sẵn sàng trong mili giây. Data stream là luồng dữ liệu liên tục (ví dụ: clickstream từ website lưu lượng cao, đọc cảm biến IoT). Kinesis Data Streams: thu thập và xử lý luồng lớn record thời gian thực; Kinesis Data Firehose: nạp dữ liệu streaming vào data store AWS (S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch) — gần thời gian thực; Kinesis Data Analytics: phân tích streaming bằng SQL hoặc Apache Flink thời gian thực.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Other Key Analytics Services
sections.contentHtml:
Amazon RedshiftFully managed data warehouse; columnar storage; for analytical (OLAP) queries on large historical datasets
AWS GlueServerless ETL service; visually create ETL workflows; includes a data catalog for metadata discovery
Amazon AthenaServerless SQL query service; run queries directly on data in S3 — no loading required
Amazon EMRManaged Hadoop framework; run Spark, Hive, HBase for big data processing; not serverless
Amazon QuickSightBusiness intelligence (BI) and data visualization; create dashboards and charts
AWS Lake FormationSimplifies setting up and securing data lakes on S3
Amazon MSKManaged Streaming for Apache Kafka; use when you already run Kafka and want a managed service
sections.contentVi: Amazon Redshift: data warehouse được quản lý hoàn toàn, lưu trữ theo cột, cho truy vấn phân tích (OLAP) trên dataset lịch sử lớn. AWS Glue: ETL serverless, tạo workflow ETL trực quan, có data catalog để khám phá metadata. Amazon Athena: SQL serverless, chạy truy vấn trực tiếp trên dữ liệu trong S3, không cần nạp dữ liệu. Amazon EMR: Hadoop được quản lý, chạy Spark/Hive/HBase cho big data, không phải serverless. Amazon QuickSight: BI và trực quan hóa dữ liệu, tạo dashboard và biểu đồ. AWS Lake Formation: đơn giản hóa thiết lập và bảo mật data lake trên S3. Amazon MSK: Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka, dùng khi đã chạy Kafka và muốn dịch vụ được quản lý.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Key Analytics Distinctions
sections.contentHtml:
• Kinesis → real-time streaming in milliseconds; IoT, clickstreams, live logs
  • Redshift → data warehouse for OLAP on historical data; not for OLTP or real-time
  • Glue → serverless ETL + data catalog; for data pipeline preparation
  • Athena → serverless SQL directly on S3; no data loading or cluster management
  • EMR → managed Hadoop/Spark; for complex big data workloads; not serverless
  • QuickSight → BI dashboards and visualization
  • MSK → managed Kafka; for existing Kafka pipelines migrating to AWS
Tip: Real-time → Kinesis. SQL on S3 without loading → Athena. Historical analytics → Redshift. ETL pipeline → Glue. Big data Hadoop/Spark → EMR.
sections.contentVi: Kinesis → streaming thời gian thực trong mili giây; IoT, clickstream, log trực tiếp. Redshift → data warehouse cho OLAP trên dữ liệu lịch sử, không dùng cho OLTP hay real-time. Glue → ETL serverless + data catalog, chuẩn bị pipeline dữ liệu. Athena → SQL serverless trực tiếp trên S3, không nạp dữ liệu hay quản lý cluster. EMR → Hadoop/Spark được quản lý cho workload big data phức tạp, không phải serverless. QuickSight → dashboard BI và trực quan hóa. MSK → Kafka được quản lý cho pipeline Kafka hiện có migration lên AWS. Mẹo: thời gian thực → Kinesis; SQL trên S3 không nạp dữ liệu → Athena; analytics lịch sử → Redshift; ETL → Glue; Hadoop/Spark → EMR.

summary: Amazon Kinesis analyzes streaming data in real time (milliseconds); use for IoT sensors, clickstreams, and live log processing
Amazon Redshift is a fully managed data warehouse (OLAP); Amazon Athena runs serverless SQL directly on S3 data without loading
AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service with a data catalog; AWS Lake Formation simplifies data lake setup and governance on S3
Amazon EMR provides managed Hadoop/Spark for big data; Amazon QuickSight handles BI dashboards; Amazon MSK is managed Apache Kafka
Data lake = centralized store for all structured and unstructured data; ETL = Extract, Transform, Load pipeline
summaryVi: Amazon Kinesis phân tích dữ liệu streaming thời gian thực (mili giây); dùng cho cảm biến IoT, clickstream và xử lý log trực tiếp
Amazon Redshift là data warehouse được quản lý (OLAP); Amazon Athena chạy SQL serverless trực tiếp trên dữ liệu S3 không cần nạp
AWS Glue là ETL serverless có data catalog; AWS Lake Formation đơn giản hóa thiết lập và quản trị data lake trên S3
Amazon EMR cung cấp Hadoop/Spark được quản lý cho big data; Amazon QuickSight xử lý dashboard BI; Amazon MSK là Apache Kafka được quản lý
Data lake = kho lưu trữ tập trung cho tất cả dữ liệu có cấu trúc và phi cấu trúc; ETL = pipeline Extract, Transform, Load

quiz.question: A company collects clickstream data from millions of users on its e-commerce website and needs to analyze user behavior in real time to personalize recommendations with sub-second latency. Which AWS service is MOST appropriate for ingesting and processing this streaming data?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon Redshift
AWS Glue
Amazon Athena
quiz.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is designed for real-time streaming data ingestion and processing with millisecond latency, making it ideal for clickstream analysis. Amazon Redshift is an OLAP data warehouse for batch analytics, AWS Glue is an ETL service, and Amazon Athena runs SQL queries on S3 data but is not a real-time streaming solution.

quiz.question: A retail company has years of historical sales data stored in Amazon S3 in Parquet format. The business intelligence team needs to run complex SQL queries across this data for quarterly reporting without provisioning or managing any servers. Which solution offers the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Load the data into Amazon Redshift and query using SQL
Use Amazon Athena to run SQL queries directly on S3
Spin up an Amazon EMR cluster with Hive to query the data
Use AWS Glue to transform and load data into RDS, then query RDS
quiz.explanation: Amazon Athena is a serverless query service that runs SQL directly on data in S3 with no infrastructure to manage, providing the least operational overhead. Redshift requires provisioning a cluster, EMR requires managing a cluster, and loading into RDS adds complexity and may not scale for large analytical workloads.

quiz.question: An IoT company streams sensor readings from 50,000 devices. They need to detect anomalies within milliseconds and trigger automated alerts. Which AWS service should they use to process this real-time data stream?
options: Amazon SQS with Lambda polling
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with Kinesis Data Analytics
Amazon EMR with Apache Spark batch jobs
AWS Glue streaming ETL jobs
quiz.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams combined with Kinesis Data Analytics provides real-time stream processing with millisecond latency, which is essential for anomaly detection from IoT sensors. SQS with Lambda introduces polling delays, EMR batch jobs are not real-time, and Glue streaming ETL has higher latency and is more suited for ETL pipelines.

quiz.question: A financial services company runs nightly batch reports aggregating billions of rows of transaction data from multiple source systems. The queries involve complex joins and aggregations. Which service is MOST suited for this OLAP workload?
options: Amazon DynamoDB
Amazon Aurora
Amazon Redshift
Amazon Athena
quiz.explanation: Amazon Redshift is a fully managed OLAP data warehouse optimized for complex analytical queries over large datasets with columnar storage and massively parallel processing. DynamoDB and Aurora are OLTP databases not optimized for complex aggregations at this scale. Athena works on S3 data and is suitable for ad-hoc queries but Redshift typically outperforms it for repeated complex analytics on structured data.

quiz.question: A data engineering team needs to build a pipeline that extracts data from an Amazon RDS database, transforms it by cleaning and enriching records, and loads it into Amazon Redshift for reporting. They want a fully managed, serverless solution. Which TWO services should they use? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Glue for the ETL transformation
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose for the ETL transformation
Amazon EMR with a custom Spark script
AWS Glue Data Catalog to store table metadata
Amazon Athena to move data into Redshift
quiz.explanation: AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that can extract from RDS, transform data, and load into Redshift with no infrastructure management. The AWS Glue Data Catalog stores and manages metadata about datasets, which is essential for a well-governed ETL pipeline. Kinesis Firehose is for streaming ingestion, not batch ETL; EMR requires cluster management; and Athena is a query service, not a data movement tool.

quiz.question: A media company wants to set up a centralized repository on Amazon S3 that stores raw video metadata, structured user engagement logs, and unstructured social media comments — all accessible to multiple analytics teams with fine-grained access control. Which approach provides the MOST scalable governance for this use case?
options: Store data in separate S3 buckets with individual bucket policies per team
Use Amazon Redshift as the central store with row-level security
Use AWS Lake Formation to build a governed data lake on S3
Use Amazon EMR with HDFS as the central data repository
quiz.explanation: AWS Lake Formation is purpose-built to simplify data lake setup and governance on S3, providing centralized access control, data cataloging, and fine-grained permissions across both structured and unstructured data. Separate S3 bucket policies are hard to manage at scale, Redshift is an OLAP warehouse not suited for raw unstructured data, and EMR with HDFS introduces operational overhead.

quiz.question: A startup wants to run big data processing jobs using Apache Spark to train machine learning models on a large dataset stored in S3. They need the flexibility to use custom libraries and only pay when the jobs run. Which AWS service is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics
AWS Glue
Amazon EMR
Amazon Redshift Spectrum
quiz.explanation: Amazon EMR provides managed Hadoop and Spark clusters with the flexibility to install custom libraries, use spot instances for cost savings, and terminate the cluster when done. Kinesis Data Analytics is for real-time stream processing, AWS Glue supports Spark but has limited customization for ML workloads, and Redshift Spectrum is for querying S3 data from Redshift, not running custom Spark jobs.

quiz.question: A business wants to create interactive dashboards showing sales KPIs for non-technical executives. The data already resides in Amazon Redshift and Amazon S3. The solution should require NO infrastructure management. Which service should they use?
options: Amazon QuickSight
Amazon Athena with a custom web frontend
Amazon EMR with Jupyter Notebooks
AWS Glue with a visualization plugin
quiz.explanation: Amazon QuickSight is a fully managed, serverless BI service that natively connects to Redshift and S3 (via Athena) to create interactive dashboards with no infrastructure to manage. Athena with a custom frontend requires significant development work, EMR with Jupyter is developer-oriented and requires cluster management, and AWS Glue is an ETL service without BI capabilities.

quiz.question: A company migrating from a self-managed Apache Kafka cluster to AWS wants to keep using the Kafka API and existing Kafka-compatible applications without rewriting code. Which AWS service should they choose with the LEAST migration effort?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon MQ
Amazon MSK
Amazon SQS FIFO
quiz.explanation: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) is a fully managed Kafka service that is API-compatible with open-source Kafka, allowing existing Kafka applications to run without code changes. Kinesis is a competing streaming service that requires code changes to migrate to. Amazon MQ supports ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ protocols, and SQS FIFO is a different messaging paradigm.

quiz.question: A data platform team is building a data lake on Amazon S3. They need to catalog all datasets so analysts can discover them, and they need a serverless way to run ad-hoc SQL queries on the cataloged data. Which TWO services should they combine? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Glue Data Catalog to register and manage dataset schemas
Amazon Redshift to store and index all S3 objects
Amazon Athena to query S3 data using the Glue catalog
Amazon EMR to provide a persistent SQL query endpoint
Amazon Kinesis to stream catalog updates
quiz.explanation: AWS Glue Data Catalog provides a centralized metadata repository that stores schema information about datasets in S3, and Amazon Athena is a serverless query engine that integrates natively with the Glue Data Catalog to run SQL queries directly on S3. Redshift requires loading data first, EMR requires cluster management and is not serverless, and Kinesis is for streaming, not cataloging or querying.

quiz.question: An e-commerce platform generates 10 GB of application logs per hour that are currently discarded. A solutions architect needs to store these logs durably, make them queryable with SQL for ad-hoc analysis, and keep costs MINIMAL. Which architecture is MOST cost-effective?
options: Stream logs to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, then store in Amazon Redshift for SQL querying
Stream logs to Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, store in S3, and query with Amazon Athena
Store logs in Amazon RDS and run SQL queries directly
Use AWS Glue to continuously load logs into Amazon DynamoDB
quiz.explanation: Kinesis Data Firehose durably delivers logs to S3 at low cost with no servers to manage, and Athena's pay-per-query model on S3 data is highly cost-effective for ad-hoc analysis. Loading into Redshift incurs higher storage and compute costs, storing in RDS would be costly and not scalable for this volume, and DynamoDB is not optimized for SQL analytics.

quiz.question: A company's data science team needs to run complex ETL jobs to process JSON files in S3, convert them to Parquet, and catalog the output schema for downstream Athena queries. They want ZERO cluster management. Which service fulfills these requirements?
options: Amazon EMR with auto-scaling enabled
AWS Glue with a crawler and ETL job
Amazon Redshift with external tables
Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics with SQL transforms
quiz.explanation: AWS Glue is a fully serverless ETL service that can run Spark-based ETL jobs to convert JSON to Parquet, and Glue crawlers automatically discover and catalog the output schema in the Glue Data Catalog for Athena. EMR requires cluster provisioning and management, Redshift external tables query data but don't transform and re-catalog it, and Kinesis Data Analytics is for real-time stream processing.

quiz.question: A gaming company stores player telemetry events — including match results, in-game purchases, and session durations — in Amazon S3 as compressed CSV files. An analyst wants to run a one-time query to find the top 100 highest-spending players without loading data anywhere. Which is the MOST cost-effective approach?
options: Provision an Amazon Redshift cluster, load all CSV data, run the query, then delete the cluster
Launch an Amazon EMR cluster with Hive, query the data, then terminate
Use Amazon Athena to run the SQL query directly on the S3 CSV files
Use AWS Glue to load the data into DynamoDB and query it
quiz.explanation: Amazon Athena is serverless and charges only per query scanned, making it ideal for one-time ad-hoc queries on S3 data with no infrastructure costs. Provisioning Redshift or EMR for a single query incurs cluster startup costs and management overhead. Loading into DynamoDB would be expensive and DynamoDB is not optimized for SQL analytical queries.

quiz.question: A company wants to build a modern data lake architecture on AWS. The requirements are: (1) all raw data from various sources must land in S3, (2) the data must be cataloged for discovery, and (3) access to sensitive datasets must be controlled at the column level. Which TWO services satisfy requirements 2 and 3? (Choose TWO)
options: AWS Glue Data Catalog for metadata management and schema discovery
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams for real-time data ingestion
AWS Lake Formation for fine-grained column-level access control
Amazon Redshift Spectrum for external table access
Amazon EMR security groups for access control
quiz.explanation: AWS Glue Data Catalog satisfies requirement 2 by cataloging datasets and enabling schema discovery. AWS Lake Formation satisfies requirement 3 by providing column-level and row-level security on top of the data catalog for fine-grained access control on S3 data. Kinesis is for ingestion (requirement 1 area), Redshift Spectrum is a query tool, and EMR security groups control network access, not data-level permissions.

quiz.question: A news organization processes live social media feeds to detect trending topics within seconds of posting. The pipeline must handle variable, high-throughput message volumes and scale automatically. Which service is BEST suited for ingesting this real-time stream?
options: Amazon SQS Standard Queue
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon MSK with a manual consumer group
AWS Glue streaming job triggered every 30 seconds
quiz.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is designed for high-throughput real-time streaming data ingestion with automatic scaling, enabling processing within milliseconds. SQS Standard does not guarantee ordering and is not optimized for real-time analytics pipelines. MSK (Kafka) is also a valid streaming platform but requires more operational management. Glue streaming with a 30-second trigger introduces latency incompatible with 'within seconds' requirements.

quiz.question: A company has petabytes of structured sales data in Amazon Redshift and wants analysts to also query historical raw data stored in S3 without duplicating data into Redshift. Which Redshift feature enables this with MINIMAL additional infrastructure?
options: Redshift Multi-AZ deployment
Redshift Spectrum
Amazon Athena federated query
AWS Glue cross-account catalog
quiz.explanation: Redshift Spectrum allows Redshift users to run SQL queries that join Redshift tables with data stored in S3 without moving or copying data, extending Redshift's query capabilities to S3 with no extra infrastructure. Athena federated query also queries S3 but is separate from Redshift. Multi-AZ is a high-availability feature, and Glue cross-account catalog manages metadata across accounts.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is designing an analytics platform. The team has data scientists who need to run custom PySpark jobs for feature engineering, but also analysts who need to run quick ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 data. Which combination provides the MOST appropriate tools for BOTH personas with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Amazon EMR for PySpark jobs and Amazon Redshift for SQL queries
Amazon EMR for both PySpark jobs and SQL queries using Hive
AWS Glue for PySpark jobs and Amazon Athena for SQL queries
Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics for PySpark jobs and Amazon QuickSight for SQL queries
quiz.explanation: AWS Glue provides serverless managed Spark (PySpark) execution for data scientists with no cluster management, and Amazon Athena provides serverless SQL querying on S3 for analysts. Both are fully managed with no servers to provision. EMR with Redshift requires cluster and warehouse management, and Kinesis Data Analytics is for stream processing, not batch PySpark; QuickSight is a BI tool, not a SQL query engine.

quiz.question: A company wants to migrate its on-premises Hadoop data warehouse to AWS. The new architecture must support both batch big data processing with custom Spark jobs AND serverless interactive SQL queries on the same S3 data. Which TWO services should be included in the architecture? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon EMR for running managed Spark batch processing jobs
Amazon Redshift for all interactive SQL queries
Amazon Athena for serverless interactive SQL queries on S3
Amazon Kinesis for batch data ingestion
AWS Database Migration Service for ongoing replication
quiz.explanation: Amazon EMR provides the managed Hadoop/Spark environment needed for custom big data batch processing, replacing the on-premises Hadoop cluster. Amazon Athena enables serverless SQL queries directly on S3 data without loading it anywhere. Redshift requires data loading and cluster management, Kinesis is for real-time streaming (not batch Hadoop replacement), and DMS is for database migration, not analytics architecture.

quiz.question: A logistics company needs to monitor truck GPS locations in real time, calculate estimated arrival times, and update a live dashboard every few seconds. The solution must handle thousands of simultaneous data producers. Which architecture is MOST appropriate?
options: Trucks write to Amazon S3, AWS Glue triggers every minute to process, Amazon QuickSight refreshes the dashboard
Trucks publish to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, a consumer application calculates ETAs, and Amazon QuickSight shows the live dashboard
Trucks write to Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon Redshift pulls data every 5 minutes, QuickSight refreshes
Trucks send data to Amazon SQS, Lambda processes batches every 30 seconds, QuickSight shows results
quiz.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams supports thousands of producers and millisecond latency, enabling a consumer application to calculate and push ETAs in real time to QuickSight. Writing to S3 with Glue, polling DynamoDB every 5 minutes, or batching with SQS/Lambda all introduce multi-second or multi-minute delays, which are incompatible with a 'live dashboard every few seconds' requirement.

quiz.question: A company stores structured customer data and unstructured product reviews in Amazon S3. Different teams — data engineers, analysts, and ML engineers — all need access, but only analysts should see the customer PII columns, and ML engineers should only access product review data. Which service BEST enforces these access boundaries NATIVELY?
options: S3 bucket policies with prefix-level restrictions for each team
Amazon Redshift row-level security policies
AWS Lake Formation with column-level and table-level permissions
AWS IAM policies with S3 object tagging for each column
quiz.explanation: AWS Lake Formation provides native column-level and table-level access control on top of a data lake in S3, allowing administrators to grant or deny access to specific columns (to hide PII) and specific tables (to restrict ML engineers to product reviews). S3 bucket policies work at the object/prefix level and cannot enforce column-level restrictions. Redshift row-level security applies only to data loaded into Redshift, and IAM with S3 tagging does not provide column-level granularity.
